actions.thought: 73 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. In the first hand of the tournament, there is no reason to play such a weak hand or attempt a speculative bluff from the Small Blind. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for better starting cards.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, the Button (SB) acts first pre-flop and has position post-flop. However, J4o is a very weak hand, ranking in the bottom 15% of starting hands. While a wide opening range (up to 80-90%) is common in heads-up, J4o typically falls into the folding range unless the opponent is known to be extremely passive. Since this is only the second hand and I have no specific reads on 'Flash', the standard and prudent play is to fold and preserve chips for hands with better playability.

8d 2h is an extremely weak hand, even with the positional advantage of being the Button post-flop. Against a professional opponent, playing such a low-equity hand is generally negative expected value (EV). I will fold and wait for a better spot to exploit my position.

In Heads-Up play, being on the Button (Small Blind) requires a wide opening range, but 6c 2c is near the very bottom of that range (approximately bottom 15% of hands). It is a suited 4-gapper with very little connectivity. While the opponent has been passive so far, playing such a weak hand early in the tournament invites unnecessary variance. Folding here is the standard play to preserve chips and maintain a solid image.

I am holding 72 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although the cost to call is only 50 chips to see a flop, this hand has very poor playability and equity. In the early stages of this 150-hand match, there is no reason to commit chips with such a weak hand, especially against an opponent labeled as 'Pro'. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button (SB) with Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o). In heads-up play, any Ace is a strong starting hand and falls well within the standard opening range. The previous hands have all resulted in the Small Blind folding, indicating a very passive start to the match. Given my position, stack depth (100BB), and holding an Ace, folding is incorrect. A min-raise to 200 serves to steal the blind potentially ending the streak of folds or building a pot where I have the advantage of position and high-card value.

I have King-Three suited (Kd 3d) in the Big Blind. In a heads-up scenario, any suited King is a strong starting hand. The opponent raised to 200 from the Small Blind, meaning I only need to call 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400. This gives me 4-to-1 pot odds, requiring only 20% equity to justify a call. K3s has significantly more equity than that against a typical button opening range. I will call to see the flop and play post-flop, as this hand is too strong to fold but doesn't necessarily warrant a 3-bet raise right now.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. As the defender out of position, checking is the standard play here. I want to control the size of the pot and see how the aggressor proceeds. My hand has some value but is very vulnerable to an Ace or any pocket pair.

I hold Top Pair with the worst kicker (Ah2d) on an As-4s-3c board. While Top Pair is strong in heads-up, my hand is vulnerable to domination by better Aces. Additionally, the board contains flush and straight draws. By checking back, I exercise pot control, avoiding a situation where I face a check-raise that would force me to fold a hand with significant showdown value. Checking also disguises the strength of my hand, potentially inducing bluffs from Flash on later streets or allowing me to catch a straight if a 5 hits. This line protects my checking range and simplifies the game tree for a marginal made hand.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker. The opponent checked back the flop, which often indicates they do not have a strong Ace or are playing a marginal hand or draw passively. The turn 7d is largely a blank. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and see how the opponent reacts, as my hand has some showdown value but cannot withstand much pressure.

I am on the Turn with Top Pair (Aces) and a weak kicker (2). The board is As-4s-3c-7d. My opponent has checked to me again. Consistent with my plan on the flop, I will check back here for pot control. Betting opens me up to a check-raise which would be disastrous for my specific hand, and I want to keep the pot small given my kicker issues. Checking also induces bluffs on the river and disguises the strength of my hand. I am happy to take this to showdown or call a reasonable bet on the river.

The river board is As-4s-3c-7d-4d. I have two pair, fours and threes, with a King kicker. My opponent raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn. This sequence typically indicates a weak range, such as missed high cards or a small pocket pair that is afraid of the Ace. By checking the river, I can see a showdown for free and potentially win against hands like KQ, KJ, or small underpairs. If I bet, I'm unlikely to get called by many worse hands, so checking is the most efficient way to realize my hand's value.

I arrive at the River with Top Pair (Aces) and a weak kicker on a board of As-4s-3c-7d-4d. The board has paired the 4, meaning my best five-card hand is A-A-4-4-7. 

Strategic breakdown:
1.  **Hand Strength**: My kicker (Deuce) does not play; the 7 on the board plays. I chop with any Ace that has a kicker lower than 8 (A2, A3, A5, A6). I lose to any Ace with an 8 or better, and I lose to any 4 (Trips) or Full House (33, 77).
2.  **Opponent Range**: Flash defended the Big Blind and has checked three streets. This line suggests weakness, likely a missed draw, a small pair (3x, 7x, 55, 66), or a weak Ace. A strong Ace likely would have bet the Turn or River for value after I showed weakness.
3.  **Value vs. Showdown**: If I bet, I only get called by worse hands (like a pair of 3s or 7s) if Flash decides to hero-call a 'bluff'. Better Aces dominate me, and random 4s beat me. By betting, I risk 'value-owning' myself against A8+ or facing a check-raise. 
4.  **Conclusion**: Given the likelihood of a chop against other weak Aces and the risk of running into a better kicker or trips, checking back is the most prudent play. It guarantees I realize my showdown value in this small pot without exposing myself to unnecessary loss.

Pocket sixes is a strong medium pair in heads-up play. Since I am on the button, I will raise to 300 (a 3x open) to take control of the hand and build value. This is significantly stronger than the hands I've folded previously from the button.

I hold Jd9d in the Big Blind facing a 3x open (300 chips) from the Button. J9s is a strong speculative hand in heads-up play, offering good playability post-flop with straight and flush potential. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600), requiring only 25% equity. J9s has roughly 46% equity against a wide opening range. While a 3-bet is possible, calling is the standard play to realize equity and keep the opponent's range wide, especially given the slightly larger opening size. I will call to see a flop.

I hold Jd9d on a K-6-4 rainbow flop. As the pre-flop caller out of position, I am at a range disadvantage on this King-high board, which favors the pre-flop raiser. My hand has no immediate draws (no flush draw, no straight draw) and is currently just Jack-high. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donking) would be effectively bluffing into a range that contains many Kings and better pairs, with very little equity to fall back on. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I have flopped a set of sixes on a relatively dry board (Ks-6h-4c). This is an extremely strong hand. My opponent checked, and I want to start building the pot to extract value from any King, straight draws like 57, or mid-pairs in their range. A half-pot bet of 300 is a standard value bet here.

I hold Jd9d on a dry K-6-4 rainbow flop. I checked to the pre-flop raiser, who has now bet 300 (half-pot). I have completely missed this board, holding only Jack-high with no straight or flush draws. While I have some backdoor potential, it is not enough to justify calling a half-pot bet out of position against a range that includes many Kings and better pairs. The opponent's range advantage on this King-high board is significant. Floating here is essentially burning chips; the disciplined play is to fold and move to the next hand.

In Heads-Up play, 97o is a marginal hand (top ~58%) that is too weak to comfortably raise for value but strong enough to play from the Button given the 3:1 pot odds and positional advantage. I have folded my previous weak buttons (J4o, 62o), so my range appears tight. Limping (completing the SB to 100) allows me to see a flop cheaply in position against an opponent who has shown passive tendencies (checked down Hand #6). If the opponent raises, I can easily fold. If they check, I can evaluate the flop with position. Folding is too tight, and raising bloats the pot with a hand that plays poorly against a calling range.

I am in the Big Blind with 87o. My opponent on the Button (Small Blind) has limped in. There is no reason to raise and build a pot with a mediocre hand out of position. Checking allows me to see a flop for free, which is the most profitable play in this situation.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a board of Td-7c-5c. In a limped pot, checking is the standard approach with a medium-strength hand like this to control the size of the pot and see how the opponent reacts. I am out of position, and there's no need to build a large pot yet.

I hold middle pair (7s) on a fairly wet board (Td 7c 5c). In a limped pot, ranges are wide. While my hand has value, it is vulnerable to overcards and draws. Betting risks facing a check-raise which would force me to fold my equity, or building a pot where I am either way ahead or way behind. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent on later streets. It also protects my checking range. Given the opponent's previous passive tendencies (checking down Hand #6), checking back to get to showdown cheaply or call a turn bet is a prudent strategy.

The turn card is a King, which is an overcard to my pair of Sevens. Given that this was a limped pot and both players checked the flop, my pair of Sevens still has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value. By checking, I can control the pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or react to my opponent's action. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse or fold out better hands.

I hold a pair of sevens (3rd pair) on a board of Td 7c 5c Kd. The pot is small (200) in a limped blind battle. The opponent has checked to me on the Flop and Turn, showing weakness or a draw. Betting here has little value as I likely only get called by better hands (Tx, Kx) and fold out worse hands that I am currently beating. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity for free, and attempt to get to showdown cheaply. I avoid being check-raised off my equity.

The river is a 2s, which is a complete blank. The board is Td-7c-5c-Kd-2s. I have a pair of Sevens (7s, 8d). This hand has significant showdown value in a limped pot where both players have checked every street. Pro's passivity suggests they likely do not have a King or a Ten. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I might get raised by a hand that was slow-playing. Checking is the best option to realize my equity and reach a showdown for free.

I hold a pair of sevens (3rd pair) on a completed board of Td 7c 5c Kd 2s. The action has been passive throughout, with both players checking the Flop and Turn. The opponent has checked to me again on the River. Betting here has very thin value; better hands (Tx, Kx) will call, and worse hands (missed draws, air) will likely fold. By checking, I guarantee a showdown to see if my hand is good against missed clubs/diamonds or random high cards without risking more chips or facing a check-raise.

Holding 62s on the button in a heads-up situation. While it is a weak hand, its suitedness provides some post-flop equity and playability. In heads-up play, the button should be opening a wide range to capitalize on positional advantage. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to put pressure on the opponent and potentially win the pot immediately. If the big blind calls, I will evaluate the flop and look to use my position to control the hand.

I hold QTo in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. In heads-up play, QTo is well above the median hand strength and has roughly 55% equity against a wide opening range. Pot odds require 33% equity to call (calling 200 to win 400). Given the price and hand strength, folding is too tight. A 3-bet is an option but bloats the pot out of position with an unsuited hand that plays reasonably well as a flat call. I will call to see a flop.

I hold QsTh on a board of AsKs5d. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and a backdoor flush draw. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, as they have all the strong Ax and Kx combos. Leading out (donk betting) is not advisable as I rarely get folds from better hands and I am out of position. The standard play is to check to the aggressor to see what they do. If they bet, I will evaluate based on sizing; if they check, I realize equity for free.

The flop of As-Ks-5d is very favorable for my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, as it contains two high cards (Ace and King). My opponent checked, which is expected. Even though I have no direct pair or strong draw with 6h-2h, this is an ideal situation for a continuation bet. A bet here puts maximum pressure on the opponent's range, which likely consists of mid-pairs or suited connectors that missed this board. I will bet 300 (half the pot) to take down the hand now; if I am called, I will likely check down the rest of the hand unless my equity improves significantly.

I hold QsTh on a board of Ks 5d As. The opponent (Flash) has bet 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot). This board texture (Ace-King) heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, as they retain all strong Ax and Kx hands, whereas I would often 3-bet strong Aces pre-flop. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Jack) and a backdoor flush draw with the Queen of spades. However, my immediate equity is low (roughly 8-9% to hit the straight on the turn), and I need 25% equity to call based on pot odds. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize implied odds or bluff effectively on later streets against a range that connects so well with the board. Floating with just a gutshot against a half-pot bet is generally negative EV in this spot. The disciplined play is to fold.

I hold 4s 3d (43 offsuit) on the Button/Small Blind. In Heads-Up play, while the Button acts last post-flop, 43o is one of the weakest starting hands (bottom 5-10%). Given that we are 100BB deep, there is little incentive to bloat the pot or try to steal with such low equity, especially since the opponent (Flash) has shown they will defend or raise. Folding is the standard, low-variance play here to preserve the stack for better spots.

I am on the button with J9o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Based on previous hands, the opponent (Pro) appears to be playing somewhat conservatively and has folded to pre-flop aggression or early post-flop bets. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately, while remaining consistent with my previous betting patterns.

I hold ATo in the Big Blind, which is a strong starting hand in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, ranking well above the median hand strength. The opponent (Flash) on the Button has raised to 3x (300), a sizing they have used previously. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400) and the strength of my hand, folding is out of the question. While a 3-bet is a valid option to take the initiative, calling is often preferred with ATo to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid isolating myself against a stronger range (like AJ+ or high pairs) while playing out of position. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate.

I hold AcTs on a flop of 7h 3s 7d. The board is paired and very dry, meaning it likely missed both of our ranges, though the Big Blind (me) has more random 7x hands in range than the pre-flop raiser. However, being out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My Ace-high has decent showdown value, and leading out (donking) would likely fold out worse hands that might bluff or call down with better hands. I will check and evaluate based on whether the opponent makes a continuation bet.

The flop of 7h-3s-7d is very dry and unlikely to have hit my opponent's calling range from the big blind. Since I have the initiative from pre-flop and my opponent has checked, a continuation bet is a high-frequency play here. I'm betting 300 into a 600 pot to apply pressure and likely take the pot down immediately, as the opponent has shown a tendency to fold to post-flop aggression in previous hands.

I hold AcTs on a paired board of 7h 3s 7d. The opponent has made a continuation bet of 300 (half pot), which matches their sizing in previous hands (#7, #9). In Heads-Up play, this board texture interacts poorly with most opening ranges, meaning Ace-high is frequently the best hand or has significant equity against overcards. I am getting 4:1 pot odds (calling 300 to win 1200), requiring only 25% equity to continue. Against a wide button opening range that c-bets frequently, my hand is too strong to fold. Raising would turn my hand into a bluff and might isolate me against better hands, whereas calling allows me to keep their bluffs in the pot and realize my showdown value. I will call.

I am out of position on the turn with the board reading 7h 3s 7d 6h. I hold Ace-Ten offsuit. I previously check-called a flop bet. The turn card (6h) brings a flush draw and connects with some lower cards, but the board remains paired. As the pre-flop caller playing out of position against an aggressor who c-bet the flop, standard strategy dictates checking to the raiser. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called by better hands (pairs, trips) and fold out the bluffs (King-high, Queen-high) that I am currently beating. By checking, I allow the opponent to either check back for a free river card or fire a second barrel, which I can then evaluate based on sizing. My goal is to get to showdown cheaply with my showdown value.

Pro's decision to call the flop bet is a significant departure from their previous behavior of folding to continuation bets (as seen in Hand #7 and Hand #9). This suggest Pro has connected with the board in some way, likely holding a pair of threes, a pocket pair, or possibly a seven. With my J-high, I have very little equity. Checking back the turn is the most logical choice to control the pot size and avoid being check-raised, while giving me a free look at the river where I might improve or decide on a different line based on the final card.

The river is the 3h, making the final board 7h 3s 7d 6h 3h. This is a double-paired board (Sevens and Threes). My hand (AcTs) plays as Two Pair with an Ace kicker (7-7-3-3-A), which is the strongest possible kicker for this board texture. I chop with any other non-paired Ace, beat all King-high or lower hands, but lose to any pocket pair 44+ or any hand containing a 6, 7, or 3. Since the opponent checked back the turn, their range is likely capped and contains many high-card hands or weak pairs. Betting here serves no purpose: worse hands like King-high will fold, and better hands like pocket pairs or boats will call. Checking is the dominant strategy to realize showdown value or induce a bluff.

The board has paired twice (7s and 3s), which means my Jack-high (Js 9c) currently plays as two pair, Sevens and Threes, with a Jack kicker. Pro called my continuation bet on the flop, which is a significant change in their behavior compared to previous hands (Hands #7 and #9) where they folded to post-flop aggression. This call suggests Pro likely holds a seven, a three, a pocket pair, or a strong Ace-high. By checking the turn and the river, Pro has shown passivity, but since my Jack kicker is only the fourth-best possible kicker (behind A, K, and Q), a value bet is unlikely to be called by worse, and a bluff is unlikely to fold out an Ace-high or a better hand that has reached showdown on this board. Checking back is the most prudent move to minimize risk and see if my Jack kicker holds up.

I am holding A4o on the button in a heads-up match. This is a strong starting hand relative to a random hand, well within the top 40% of hands. My opponent has been somewhat aggressive, but yielding the button with an Ace is too passive. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the blinds immediately, and play the hand in position if called. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size to apply pressure without risking too many chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. Pro has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. T9o is a solid defending hand in a heads-up situation, and I have enough chips to play the hand post-flop. I will call to see the flop.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten kicker on a very dry board (9c-4s-3h). I am first to act post-flop. In heads-up play, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. If Pro bets, I plan to call to keep their bluffs and weaker ranges in the pot. If Pro checks behind, I will likely lead on the turn for value.

I hold middle pair (4s) with an Ace kicker on a dry, rainbow board of 9c-4s-3h. This is a hand with good showdown value but not a monster. In heads-up play, second pair is often the best hand, but betting here opens me up to a check-raise which would put me in a difficult spot. Betting also folds out many worse hands (like random overcards) that I am currently beating, whereas checking keeps the opponent's range wide and may induce bluffs on the turn or river. Additionally, checking back protects my checking range. I plan to exercise pot control and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on a board of 9-4-3-8. After Pro checked back on the flop, it is likely they don't have an Overpair or a strong Ace, or they are playing very cautiously. By betting now on the turn, I can extract value from hands that picked up equity (like 8x or straight draws) and mid-pocket pairs (55-77) that might have checked behind. A bet of 200 (half the pot) is a standard sizing to build value while my hand is likely the best.

I hold a pair of Fours with an Ace kicker (3rd pair on board) on a turn of [9c 4s 3h 8c]. I checked back the flop to control the pot, and now the opponent leads for half pot (200 into 400). This bet is frequently a probe bet attempting to capitalize on my perceived weakness. My hand has showdown value and beats all missed draws (club draws, straight draws like 56/67) and pure bluffs. Folding here would be exploitable as it allows the opponent to auto-profit by betting any two cards after a flop check-back. The price of 200 to win a total pot of 800 (after call) offers 3:1 odds, requiring only 25% equity. My hand likely has sufficient equity against a wide Big Blind defense range. I will call and re-evaluate on the river.

I have Trip Nines with a Ten kicker on a board of 9-4-3-8-9. My check on the flop and bet on the turn represented a hand with some value, and Pro's call on the turn suggests they have a marginal made hand (like an 8 or a pocket pair) or a draw that has now missed. The river Nine is a great card for me as it likely gives me the best hand. Since Pro checked back on the flop, it is less likely they hold a Nine with a better kicker or a full house. I will bet 400 (half the pot) to extract value from an 8 or a pocket pair that Pro might be unwilling to fold in a heads-up pot.

The river 9h pairs the board, making the final board 9c-4s-3h-8c-9h. This runout significantly weakens my hand (A4o). My Two Pair (Nines and Fours with Ace kicker) now loses to any 8x (which makes Nines and Eights), any 9x (Trip Nines), and any pocket pair higher than 44 (55+). The opponent's line—checking the flop, betting the turn when the 8 appeared, and barreling the river—strongly suggests he either improved on the turn with an 8 or is holding a 9. While the pot odds are favorable (requiring only 25% equity to call), the opponent's Big Blind defending range contains a high density of 8x and 9x combos. My hand is a pure bluff-catcher that only beats missed draws (like clubs) or total air. Since I likely have better hands in my range to defend with (such as 8x or 9x), folding the bottom of my range here is the disciplined decision to avoid bleeding chips against value bets.

I am on the button with 6s5s, which is a strong speculative hand in heads-up play. My standard opening raise size so far has been 3x (300 total), and I want to maintain consistency while playing a hand that has good post-flop playability and potential to realize equity. Pro has shown a tendency to fold to aggression, so building the pot now allows me to take the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 3h, an extremely weak hand (43o). My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3x the big blind) from the Button. While heads-up play requires defending the Big Blind frequently, 43o falls near the very bottom of my range and performs poorly against any reasonable opening range. Getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) requires about 33% equity, which this hand barely has raw, but realizing that equity out of position with such low playability is nearly impossible. Against a 3x raise size, this is a standard fold.

In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, Ten-Nine offsuit is a playable hand from the button (Small Blind). It has reasonable connectivity and high-card value. Raising to 200 (min-raise) puts pressure on the Big Blind, denies free equity to random weak hands, and allows me to play the pot in position with the initiative. I have previously raised with Ax hands, so raising here balances my range. Limping is an option, but raising is generally preferred to build a pot with a decent hand and take down the blinds immediately if the opponent folds.

I am holding 4s 5s in the big blind. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200, meaning I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. The pot is currently 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds on the call. 4-5 suited is a solid speculative hand that plays well if it hits a flop. While I will be out of position, the price is too attractive to fold, and I prefer calling over raising to keep the pot small until I connect with the board.

On a board of 8d-Td-7s, I have a gutshot straight draw needing a 6 to complete a 4-5-6-7-8 straight. This board is very coordinated and likely hits the pre-flop raiser's range (Pro). As I am out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor and see how they react. I have some equity with my draw, but I don't want to bloat the pot yet or get blown off the hand by a large re-raise if I lead out.

I have flopped Top Pair (Tens) accompanied by an Open-Ended Straight Draw on a wet, connected board of [8d, Td, 7s]. My holding [9s, Th] significantly blocks the made straights (J9, 96). Due to the dynamic board texture offering flush and straight draws, checking back is risky as it grants free equity to overcards and draws. I choose to bet half the pot (200) to extract value from worse pairs (8x, 7x) and draws, while protecting my equity.

I have a gutshot straight draw (4-5-6-7-8) on an extremely coordinated board of 8-10-7. While I am getting 3-to-1 odds to call, several factors make this a fold: first, a 6 would complete my straight but also create a 6-7-8-10 sequence where any 9 in Pro's range would hold a higher straight. Second, the board texture (two diamonds and high connectors) hits a Button opening range very hard, and I have no other equity like a flush draw or overcards. Calling here would likely lead to difficult decisions on later streets with the low end of a possible straight.

I am on the button with QTs, a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. My strategy so far has consistently involved raising to 300 from the small blind with a wide range of hands to put pressure on my opponent and take control of the pot. Given the strength of QTs, a standard 3x raise is appropriate here to build the pot and maintain my aggressive image.

The opponent, Flash, has established a pattern of opening to 3x (300 chips) from the Button. I am in the Big Blind with Pocket Deuces (2s 2d) and need to call 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, offering 2:1 direct pot odds. While 22 is a difficult hand to play post-flop out of position, the stacks are deep (approx. 100 BB), providing excellent implied odds to set-mine. If I hit a set (approx. 12% frequency), I can likely extract significant value from an aggressive opponent who c-bets frequently. If I miss and face resistance, I can easily fold. Defending with pairs heads-up is generally standard to prevent over-folding.

I hold Pocket Deuces on a flop of 8c Kd Ks. This is a very poor texture for my hand; the board is paired with high cards, meaning my pair is an underpair and very vulnerable. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. Leading out (donk betting) serves no purpose as I would only be called by better hands. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold given the strength of their range on this board and my hand's lack of potential, but checking gives me the best chance to see a cheap showdown or turn card.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board has come out K-K-8, which is a very dry, paired board. This board texture favors the pre-flop raiser's range significantly. I have Queen-high with a backdoor diamond draw. A standard continuation bet here should put a lot of pressure on my opponent's non-King hands, such as pocket pairs below Kings or various Ace-high and high-card hands that missed. Betting 300 (half pot) is a balanced sizing that allows me to represent strength while keeping the pot manageable if I am called.

I hold Pocket Deuces (2s 2d) on a flop of 8c Kd Ks. My opponent, Flash, has continued their aggression with a bet of 300 into a pot of 600. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Kings (Kx) and high pocket pairs. My hand is an underpair and is effectively currently the worst made hand possible, losing to any higher pair, any 8, and drawing nearly dead against a King. Even against purely unpaired overcards (like QJ or AQ), I am vulnerable to being counterfeited or outdrawn on the turn and river. Being out of position with the bottom of my range and facing a bet, I have very low playability and cannot profitably continue. Folding is the correct decision.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with T3s. My stack is 96.5 BB.
